narrative:
Let's take a moment to understand the prediction made by the model regarding your application. The model predicted that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your university admission application. This prediction was based on several factors related to your academic performance, family background, and other personal attributes.

To help you understand what could change this prediction, we use something called counterfactual scenarios. These are "what if" situations that show how altering certain aspects of your profile could lead to a different outcome, specifically predicting that you would pass the exam. Essentially, they highlight the minimum changes needed to flip the prediction from failing to passing.

In your case, the model considered several features. Some of the most significant ones were your academic deciles, family income, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and whether you plan to work full-time or part-time. Among these, your academic performance in Year 1 and Year 3, as well as your undergraduate GPA, were notably below the average of other students who typically pass. Additionally, your LSAT score and family income were also lower than average.

When we look at the counterfactual scenarios, certain patterns emerge. For instance, improving your undergraduate GPA and LSAT score consistently appeared as necessary changes across different scenarios. Additionally, moving from full-time to part-time work status was another common adjustment that could positively impact the prediction. These changes often worked together, suggesting that a combination of academic improvement and reduced work commitments could be effective.

In total, several counterfactual scenarios were generated, and they consistently highlighted the importance of improving academic performance and adjusting work commitments. Specifically, raising your undergraduate GPA closer to the average of 3.21 and increasing your LSAT score towards the average of 36.70 could significantly enhance your chances. Additionally, considering part-time work instead of full-time could provide more time for study, which might improve your academic outcomes.

While some changes, like improving your GPA and LSAT score, require time and effort, they are realistic goals with dedicated preparation and support. Adjusting your work schedule might also be feasible, depending on your personal circumstances. By focusing on these areas, you can work towards a more favorable prediction and potentially secure your university admission in the future. Remember, these insights are meant to guide you, and with determination, you can make meaningful progress.
